speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape seven, with Stephanie Heller. Stephanie, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is our mother and us twins, Annetta and Stephanie. But to be truthful, I don't know which is me and which is my sister. We always quarreled about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have been probably four years old here. So it would have been '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to have this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody had pictures from our family, and they gave them to us-- you know, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I have made a mistake. I gave you first a picture which we were a bit older. We must have been about three here. And I can't tell you really which was Annetta, which is me. I remember now how we have these pictures from the family. Before mother was leaving the flat, she gave a suitcase with some photographs and something to a neighbor-- Christian neighbor-- and they gave it to us when we came back. So that's how I have some memories--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --some pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephanie, who is this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is my father, Arthur Heilbrunn, when he was a-- a tourist guide. That is one of the cars that he was driving with the people. He was not chauffeur. He was just talking to the people. And I think this would have been in 1930, in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here is when we were about 15, I think, and before Hitler came. Or was it just when he came? And it was the last picture, really, that our Mother has had. I don't know. I think Annetta is on the-- down below, I'm the higher one. But again, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- were these-- was this photo-- sorry-- a part of the group that was left with the neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right-- all those pictures, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --these photos, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this picture, I know for sure that this is Annetta. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there at that-- the moment. And [NON-ENGLISH], that was [NON-ENGLISH] Elizabeth, our little sister. See how chubby, beautiful little girl she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mom looks very worried, because that is at the time when we were already-- she didn't have any job, and everything started to look very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, I think, the last picture of this album mother has seen. I think it must have been made for some special occasion. That was just in '41, before our parents were taken away, in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I think that Annetta is down on the right, and I'm on the left, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my first husband and all, Egon Kunewalder. That is our wedding picture. Those flowers were actually smuggled to the photographer. We were not allowed to have photograph taken, and we were not allowed to have flowers. But somebody helped me to have this. And it was a lilac. And I-- [LAUGHS] --I like lilac ever since. Egon was a very good looking boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to have this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo, I really don't know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --where-- I can't remember how I got this photo. I don't know. Somebody has given it to me. I must have left it. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when my parents were not there anymore, and we were just married at the registry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --this photograph taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this here is in '47, end of '47, when we finished our training. That is the diploma we have got in the nursing school. We were photographed all together. And on the right is me, and on the left is Annetta. But again, we are quite often uncertain which one is which. But I'm-- I'm sure that this is me on right. This is a picture of our uniting, when we united here in Australia. On the right side is George Able, Annetta's husband. His Czech name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And next to an Annetta is my husband Robert, and I am on the left. We have been somewhere, at some bar mitzvah or something-- celebration, and we have been together. That time we were still young. That is in '53. Sorry, sorry, I made a mistake-- '63-- '63.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Can you tell me about the-- this photo and the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It is our big family. Since we were united here in Australia, Annetta's family grew, and my family grew. Well, I will tell you. On the left is Annetta, in this spotted dress. And behind her is her oldest son, Michael Able. Unfortunately, her husband was already dead at that time. It's 15 years ago that he died. Behind Annetta is my husband, Robert. Next to him is a daughter-in-law of Annetta's, Antonia, who is married to Annetta's second son, Danny. Next to Annetta his her grandson called Mark. Next to Mark is the girl in dark with a-- a necklace, is Daphne, who is the same age as Annetta. And she lives in Israel-- and her three sons. Behind-- next to her is Annetta's second daughter-in-law, Allison, who is married to Michael, behind Annetta. Behind her is our son, who doesn't look very much there, because you can't see him, our son, John. Next to him is his wife, Jenny. And I will show you pictures later, because they were not here in the morning. They live in Brisbane. In front of the daughter-in-law of Annetta's, is a mother-in-law of Annetta's daughter. She lives in Israel. Next to her is a grandson of Annetta, who lives in Israel too. That's the son of her daughter. Behind him, the gentlemen who holds somebody on his neck, is my son-in-law, Ellie, holding little Tal, my grandson. Next to him is husband of Daphne, Annetta's daughter and Annetta's son-in-law. Next to him is Annetta's second son, Daniel, who lives in Melbourne. In front of him is me. And next to me, the nice girl, is Rebecca, Annetta's only granddaughter. She has six grandsons and one granddaughter. I have got four granddaughters-- granddaughters and three grandsons. In front of me, sitting down, is Annetta's grandson. Next to him is my grandson, Ariel. Then is my grandson, Daniel. Naomi is holding Daniel. Next to her is this grandson of Annetta, who lives in Israel, Gil. And there is little Russ, who is grandson of Annetta. And Jordan is the son of Michael, behind Annetta. So you see all the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is my son, our son, John, and his wife, Jenny. And they live in Brisbane. And we are missing them very much. We see them occasionally, because we go there or they come here. But it is not the same as like with Naomi, who lives around the corner. We miss them very much, but we love them just the same. These are my granddaughters living in Brisbane, John's and Jenny's daughters. On the left, the big beauty is Elise-Regina. She's holding little Amy, who will be two this Christmas. She was born actually in Brisbane. The other two girls, Louise is on the right, she and Elise were born here in Melbourne when they lived here. And we miss them greatly, and they talk to us on the telephone quite often. And they always ask us when we come. And we tell them when we come too. And they tell us about their doings, and so on. The telephone bills are quite high on both sides. But we are missing them very much. They will come this Christmas to see us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, Stephanie, I'd like to thank you very much. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much for giving me the time to be able to put it all on paper. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
